data.title: Organigram to buy Berlin-based Sanity Group in €113.4M upfront deal, expanding cannabis supply network
data.description:
• Organigram Global (OGI) to acquire Berlin-based Sanity Group for up-front consideration of €113.4 million plus a maximum earnout of up to €113.8 million tied to financial performance, the company said on Wednesday, expanding its network of supply-chain partners.
  • Sanity Group operates Europe’s first two legal cannabis specialty stores as part of scientific pilot projects in Switzerland, and is strategically expanding its European footprint beyond Germany and Switzerland including Poland, the UK, the UK, and Czechia.
  • The acquisition is expected to be financed by a combination of cash on hand, proceeds from a new credit facility, and an expected C$65.2 million equity investment by BAT.
  • The German medical cannabis market was valued at over €2 billion in 2025, serving approximately 800,000 patients. The market is forecast to surpass €4.5 billion by 2028, expecting a 50% year-over-year growth rate.

data.title: Nano Nuclear outlines 2030 KRONOS prototype target while advancing global partnerships and fuel supply integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nano Nuclear Energy Inc. (NNE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Jiang Yu, Founder, Executive Chairman, President, Secretary & Treasurer, reported that Nano Nuclear is advancing its KRONOS MMR, a high-temperature gas-cooled microreactor, towards licensing and construction, highlighting "the compact modular design of our KRONOS MMR system to support factory fabrication, repeatable construction and learnings that can accelerate deployment time lines and drive cost efficiencies over time." Yu also emphasized progress at the University of Illinois site and the signing of a formal MOU with the Board of Trustees, as well as a $6.8 million incentive award from the State of Illinois.
  • Yu stated the company has "raised gross proceeds of $400 million through an October private placement," strengthening the balance sheet and extending its operational runway. He added, "Nano saw a growing interest from potential strategic partners, highlighted by a recent MOU with DS Dansuk to explore localization, manufacturing and deployment opportunities for KRONOS reactors in South Korea and the broader Asian region."
  • James Walker, CEO & Director, spoke about the progress at the University of Illinois prototype project, noting "we've completed site characterization and drilling and also signed an MOU with U. of I's Board of Trustees to outline the next steps for the design, construction, ownership and operation of our KRONOS MMR system on campus." Walker detailed ongoing procurement negotiations for key components and stated the company aims "to begin construction in mid- to late 2027 and see a realizable road map to a full-scale prototype online in or around 2030."
  • Walker described the collaboration with DS Dansuk as a path to "meaningfully derisk regulatory licensing as well as accelerate site identification and project development, facilitate introductions to prospective customers and support localization of manufacturing and component production within South Korea."
  • Jaisun Garcha, Chief Financial Officer, reported, "Our overall cash position increased significantly during the quarter, ending the period with cash and cash equivalents of $577.5 million. This was an approximate $374 million increase during the quarter ended December 31, driven by the net proceeds of our successful October 2025 private placement."

Outlook

  • Management outlined that "we are targeting submission of a construction permit application to the NRC in the coming months to formally begin the U.S. lysis process. This submission will represent a key milestone that could set the stage for initial construction at the University of Illinois in mid- to late 2027."
  • Walker explained the timeline for the University of Illinois project, stating "we aim to begin construction in mid- to late 2027 and see a realizable road map to a full-scale prototype online in or around 2030."
  • The company expects "progress towards regulatory licensing of KRONOS in the U.S. and Canada" and anticipates "potential for several commercial announcements this year, reflecting growing interest in our KRONOS MMR from customers in several markets."

Financial Results

  • Garcha stated, "Q1 loss from operations was $11.6 million. The higher year-over-year loss resulted from an approximate $8 million increase in operating expenses."
  • "Q1 net loss totaled $6.5 million, up approximately $3 million from the comparable prior year period. The net loss was lower than the loss from operations as we earned approximately $5 million of interest income on our larger cash balance."
  • "Net cash used in operating activities increased by approximately $1 million from the prior year period to $4 million. Net cash used in investing activities totaled $3.1 million and included payments for our Oak Brook, Illinois engineering facility."

Q&A

  • Sameer Joshi, H.C. Wainwright & Co, LLC, Research Division: Asked about milestones or catalysts regarding the DS Dansuk alliance. Walker responded that "you're going to see more engagement with us and DS Dansuk. You're going to see that MOU advancing into more critical planning stages" and discussed plans for a centralized manufacturing facility and additional partnerships for graphite and fuel supply in Korea.
  • Joshi also inquired about EPC partnerships in the U.S. and North America. Walker answered, "we have made a few announcements as we begin to look at how we deploy these things, how it gets coordinated. That is obviously a very separate thing to DS Dansuk where they are going to be an industrial factory partner. So they wouldn't be doing EPC. But those EPC contractors in the U.S. are going to be very important."
  • Joshi asked if the construction permit is on track for the first half of the year. Walker confirmed, "It is on track for the first half of this year. It's actually going very well at the moment."
  • Nate Pendleton questioned ways to accelerate the 2030 timeline for the UIUC project. Walker highlighted recent government pressure on the NRC to expedite timelines and said, "the 2030 time line could be expedited. It's certainly possible. But it's prudent, I think, for us to stick to that because there is a -- there's been a tendency in recent years of companies to make very ambitious date targets."
  • Jeffrey Grampp, Northland Capital Markets, Research Division: Asked about supply chain challenges. Walker responded, "the vast majority of components are not that specialized...there are actually components though that are the longer lead items. So there's a number -- like our reactor and a number of other reactors use like nuclear-grade graphite...The other major part for the U.S. is the fuel supply."
  • Sherif Elmaghrabi, BTIG: Asked about regulatory processes for LEU versus HALEU fuel. Walker explained, "when we do get the reactor licensed, we'll almost certainly get it licensed so we can demonstrate that it could operate with HALEU fuel."
  • Subhasish Chandra, The Benchmark Company, LLC: Asked about NRC licensing for balance of plant. Walker explained, "most of the KRONOS MMR system is not a nuclear system...The NRC mostly care about safety systems, how safe a reactor is."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on timelines, supply chain risks, and strategic partnerships, with a slightly positive but cautious tone. Questions probed for detail on execution risks and regulatory hurdles but acknowledged progress.
  • Management tone remained confident during prepared remarks, emphasizing achievements and strong funding, but became more measured and occasionally defensive in the Q&A, especially regarding regulatory timelines and supply chain concerns. Walker stated, "it's prudent, I think, for us to stick to that because there is a -- there's been a tendency in recent years of companies to make very ambitious date targets."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone shifted from general progress to specific questions about execution, bottlenecks, and partnerships, reflecting deeper scrutiny as timelines approach. Management's tone remained optimistic but was more explicit about risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter saw increased emphasis on vertical integration of the nuclear fuel supply chain, specifically addressing graphite and fuel availability, compared to a broader focus in the prior quarter.
  • Management provided more concrete timelines and milestones for the KRONOS MMR prototype, whereas the previous quarter focused on regulatory positioning and early-stage licensing.
  • Analysts in the current quarter probed more on execution details and supply chain risks, versus broader strategy and regulatory progress previously.
  • Management's confidence in commercial and international partnerships, such as with DS Dansuk and Ameresco, was more pronounced in the current quarter.
  • The financial position improved substantially, with the cash balance increase from $203.3 million at year-end 2025 to $577.5 million at Q1 2026.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified nuclear-grade graphite and fuel supply as key supply chain bottlenecks, noting the limited number of producers and long ramp-up times for new capacity.
  • Regulatory timelines and licensing remain a challenge, with Walker emphasizing prudence in timeline projections despite potential government pressure for expedited review.
  • Analysts raised concerns about mass manufacturing readiness, critical path items, and regulatory hurdles for both LEU and HALEU fuel.
  • Management discussed ongoing efforts to secure long-lead items, invest in supply chain partnerships, and maintain flexibility in fuel strategy to mitigate these risks.

Final Takeaway

Nano Nuclear Energy highlighted significant progress in advancing the KRONOS MMR microreactor toward licensing and construction, underpinned by strengthened finances and a growing network of strategic partnerships. Management reaffirmed its focus on vertical integration of the nuclear fuel supply chain and outlined concrete milestones, including a 2030 target for the University of Illinois prototype. The company acknowledged supply chain and regulatory challenges, emphasizing ongoing mitigation efforts and prudent timeline management as it seeks to capitalize on increasing demand for reliable, scalable nuclear power solutions in a rapidly evolving energy landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Global-E Online Non-GAAP EPS of $0.49 beats by $0.09, revenue of $336.7M beats by $8.76M
data.description:
• Global-E Online press release (GLBE): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.49 beats by $0.09.
  • Revenue of $336.7M (+28% Y/Y)  beats by $8.76M.
  • Revenue in the fourth quarter of 2025 was $336.7 million, an increase of 28% year over year, of which service fees revenue was $160.9 million and fulfillment services revenue was $175.7 million
  • GMV in the fourth quarter of 2025 was $2,361 million, an increase of 37.8% year over year

  • Global-e is introducing first quarter and full year guidance as follows:

        Q1 2026   FY 2026
        (in millions)
    GMV(1) $1,705 - $1,745   $8,450 - $8,800
    Revenue $247 - $254 vs $231.86M consensus   $1,211 - $1,271 vs $1.18B consensus
    Adjusted EBITDA(3) $46.5 - $49.5   $259 - $284
